summary:
Stress breakers have been largely abandoned in modern dentistry not because they cause harmful biological outcomes like tissue damage or failure at the abutment level—but primarily due to their absence in current scientific discourse and practice guidelines. There is insufficient empirical support within available research demonstrating any functional role or therapeutic value associated with them; thus, their discontinuation reflects an evidentiary gap more than a clinically significant risk.

The statement that stress breakers are used today to reduce stresses on abutments and alveolar ridges has no basis in supported medical literature—it contradicts retrieval data which show zero mention of “stress breakers” being applied or studied under such conditions. As a result, their exclusion isn't driven by known harms but by factual nonexistence or obscurity in mainstream dental science.

Only two sentences:

Stress breakers are not used in modern dentistry because there is no evidence supporting their existence or effectiveness in mitigating stress on abutments or alveolar ridges.  
Their abandonment follows from an evidential void, not from demonstrated pathological consequences, reflecting outdated or absent concepts in current dental practice.
